Lesson 0 - Getting Started

What computer do you use?

Chromebook

Chromebooks are inexpensive laptops that run Chrome OS, but they are not great for web development. It is possible to install Linux on some Chromebooks, but Google makes it difficult to do this without opening the computer up and making changes to the hardware. This will, at the very least, void your warranty and I honestly do not recommend doing this. I destroyed one Chromebook when I tried. If you want a cheap laptop for web development, consider a Windows or Linux machine instead.

Android

Android phones and tablets are not ideal for web development. While there are some code editors available for Android, the experience is limited compared to using a laptop or desktop computer. If possible, use a Windows, or Linux machine for the best experience.

iOS (iPhone/iPad)

Apple iPhones and iPads are not ideal for web development. While there are some code editors available for iOS, the experience is limited compared to using a laptop or desktop computer. If possible, use a macOS machine such as an iMac or MacBook for the best experience.

Introduction

In this lesson, you will ensure that you have all the necessary tools installed on your computer to start learning web development. By the end of this lesson, you will be ready to create your first web page. If you are at a Code Dojo and are using a club computer, these tools will already be installed for you so you can skip to the next lesson.

Warning

This lesson involves installing software on your computer. If you are using a shared or school computer, please check with a parent, guardian, or teacher before proceeding with the installations. Also, you will need administrative privileges to install the software. This means you will need an administrator password at various stages. If you are not using your own computer, you may need to get the owner to do those steps for you. Finally be aware that the instructions are long and detailed to ensure you get everything set up correctly. Take your time and follow each step carefully. Ask for help from a mentor, if one is available. The rest of the lessons are much shorter and easier once you have the tools installed!

Installing Required Software

These are the tools you need to install. Some may already be installed on your computer, in which case you can skip those sections. You may use other tools if you prefer, but these are the ones the lessons will assume you have.

  • Microsoft Visual Studio Code (or another code editor).
  • Mozilla Firefox (or another modern web browser such as Google Chrome or Apple Safari).
  • Git (optional, for version control, used in later lessons).
  • Node.js (required for running the development server).
  • SQLite (optional, for database management, used in later lessons).

Node.js is required from the start to run the development server. If you don't want to install Git or SQLite right now, that's okay; you can install them later when you get to the lessons that need them.

Windows Installation Instructions

The latest version of Windows is Windows 11, but you should be able to use Windows 10 as well. The lessons have not been tested on earlier versions of Windows, but they may work ok. It's possible that the browser on older versions of windows won't run all the features used in the lessons. In particular, beware of using Internet Explorer - it is not supported any more and Microsoft Edge is recommended instead. On the next screens are the instructions for installing the recommended software on your Windows computer.

Visual Studio Code

Check whether Visual Studio Code is already installed

  • Press Windows key + R
  • Type code and press Enter
  • If VS Code opens, you have it installed. If you get an error message, you need to install it.

If you need to install Visual Studio Code, follow these steps

  1. Download VS Code from the Official Website
    • Open your web browser
    • Go to https://code.visualstudio.com
    • Click the large blue "Download for Windows" button
    • The file VSCodeUserSetup-x64-[version].exe will download (usually to your Downloads folder)
  2. Run the Installer
    • Open your Downloads folder (press Windows key + E, then click Downloads)
    • Double-click the VSCodeUserSetup file you just downloaded
    • If Windows asks "Do you want to allow this app to make changes?", click Yes
  3. Setup Wizard
    • Accept the license agreement, then click Next
    • Choose installation location (default is fine), click Next
    • Choose Start Menu folder (default is fine), click Next
  4. Select Additional Tasks
    • Check these recommended options
      • Create a desktop icon
      • Add "Open with Code" action to Windows Explorer file context menu
      • Add "Open with Code" action to Windows Explorer directory context menu
      • Register Code as an editor for supported file types
      • Add to PATH
    • Click Next
  5. Install
    • Review your choices, then click Install
    • Wait for installation to complete (takes about 1-2 minutes)
    • Check "Launch Visual Studio Code", then click Finish
  6. First Launch
    • VS Code opens with a Welcome screen
    • You can choose your colour theme (light or dark)
    • You're ready to start coding!

Mozilla Firefox

Check whether Firefox is already installed

  • Press Windows key
  • Type Firefox
  • If Firefox appears in the search results, you have it installed. If not, you need to install it.

If you need to install Firefox, follow these steps

  1. Download Firefox from the Official Website
    • Open your web browser (Microsoft Edge or another browser)
    • Go to https://www.mozilla.org/firefox/
    • Click the blue "Download Firefox" button
    • The file Firefox Installer.exe will download (usually to your Downloads folder)
  2. Run the Installer
    • Open your Downloads folder (press Windows key + E, then click Downloads)
    • Double-click the Firefox Installer.exe file you just downloaded
    • If Windows asks "Do you want to allow this app to make changes?", click Yes
  3. Installation Process
    • The installer will start automatically
    • Firefox will install to the default location (usually C:\Program Files\Mozilla Firefox)
    • Wait for installation to complete (takes about 30 seconds to 1 minute)
    • Firefox will automatically launch when installation is complete
  4. First Launch
    • Firefox opens with a Welcome screen
    • You can choose to set Firefox as your default browser (optional)
    • You can import bookmarks and settings from other browsers (optional)
    • You're ready to browse!
  5. Pin to Taskbar (Optional)
    • With Firefox running, right-click the Firefox icon in the taskbar
    • Select "Pin to taskbar"
    • Now you can launch Firefox quickly from the taskbar
  6. Verify Installation
    • In Firefox, click the menu button (three horizontal lines) in the top-right corner
    • Click "Help" → "About Firefox"
    • You should see the Firefox version number

Node.js

Check whether Node.js is already installed

  • Press Windows key + R
  • Type cmd and press Enter to open Command Prompt
  • Type node --version and press Enter
  • If you see a version number, you have it installed. If you get an error message, you need to install it.

If you need to install Node.js, follow these steps

  1. Download Node.js from the Official Website
    • Open your web browser
    • Go to https://nodejs.org/
    • You'll see two download options
      • LTS (Long Term Support) - Recommended for most users
      • Current - Latest features, but may be less stable
    • Click the green button for LTS
    • The file node-v[version]-x64.msi will download (usually to your Downloads folder)
  2. Run the Installer
    • Open your Downloads folder (press Windows key + E, then click Downloads)
    • Double-click the Node.js installer file you just downloaded
    • If Windows asks "Do you want to allow this app to make changes?", click Yes
  3. Setup Wizard
    • Click "Next" on the welcome screen
    • Check "I accept the terms in the License Agreement"
    • Click "Next"
    • Default location is fine (usually C:\Program Files\nodejs\)
    • Click "Next"
    • Keep all default features selected
      • Node.js runtime
      • npm package manager
      • Online documentation shortcuts
      • Add to PATH
    • Click "Next"
    • You can optionally check "Automatically install the necessary tools" (this installs Python and Visual Studio Build Tools)
    • For basic web development, you can skip this
    • Click "Next"
  4. Install
    • Click "Install"
    • Wait for installation to complete (takes about 1-2 minutes)
    • Click "Finish"
  5. Verify Installation
    • Close any open Command Prompt windows
    • Press Windows key + R, type cmd, press Enter
    • Check Node.js version: 
      
node --version
    • You should see the Node.js version number (e.g., v20.x.x)
    • Check npm (Node Package Manager) version 
      
npm --version
    • You should see the npm version number
  6. Test Node.js (Optional)
    • In Command Prompt, type 
      
node -e "console.log('Node.js is working!')"
    • You should see "Node.js is working!" printed
  7. Create a Simple Test Server (Optional)
    • Create a test file and start a server 
      
mkdir C:\test-nodejs
cd C:\test-nodejs
echo const http = require('http'); http.createServer((req, res) => {res.writeHead(200, {'Content-Type': 'text/html'});res.end('<h1>Hello from Node.js!</h1>'); }).listen(8000, () => console.log('Server running at http://localhost:8000/')); > server.js
node server.js
    • Open your browser and go to http://localhost:8000
    • You should see "Hello from Node.js!"
    • Press Ctrl + C in Command Prompt to stop the server

Git

Check whether Git is already installed

  • Press Windows key + R
  • Type cmd and press Enter to open Command Prompt
  • Type git --version and press Enter
  • If you see a version number, you have it installed. If you get an error message, you need to install it.

If you need to install Git, follow these steps:

  1. Download Git from the Official Website
    • Open your web browser
    • Go to https://git-scm.com/
    • Click the "Download for Windows" button
    • The file Git-[version]-64-bit.exe will download (usually to your Downloads folder)
  2. Run the Installer
    • Open your Downloads folder (press Windows key + E, then click Downloads)
    • Double-click the Git installer file you just downloaded
    • If Windows asks "Do you want to allow this app to make changes?", click Yes
  3. Setup Wizard
    • Read the GNU General Public License
    • Click Next
    • Set install location. Default is fine (C:\Program Files\Git)
    • Click Next
    • Keep the default selections (recommended)
      • Additional icons (On the Desktop)
      • Git Bash Here
      • Git GUI Here
      • Associate .git* configuration files
      • Associate .sh files to be run with Bash
    • Click Next
    • Set Start Menu Folder. Default is fine ("Git")
    • Click Next
    • Select "Use Visual Studio Code as Git's default editor" from the dropdown
    • Click Next
    • Select "Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software" (recommended)
    • Click Next
    • For all remaining screens, keep the default settings
    • Click Next through each screen
    • Finally, click Install
    • Wait for installation to complete (takes about 1-2 minutes)
    • Uncheck "View Release Notes" and click Finish
  4. Configure Git
    • Press Windows key + R
    • Type cmd and press Enter
    • Set your name and email address (replace "Your Name" and "your.email@example.com" with your actual name and email): 
      
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your.email@example.com"
    • These settings will be used to identify you in your Git commits
  5. Verify Installation
    • In the same Command Prompt, type: 
      
git --version
    • You should see the Git version number
    • Check your configuration 
      
git config --global --list
    • You should see your name and email listed

SQLite

Check whether SQLite is already installed

  • Press Windows key + R
  • Type cmd and press Enter to open Command Prompt
  • Type sqlite3 --version and press Enter
  • If you see a version number, you have it installed. If you get an error message, you need to install it.

If you need to install SQLite, follow these steps

  1. Download SQLite from the Official Website
    • Open your web browser
    • Go to https://www.sqlite.org/download.html
    • Scroll down to the "Precompiled Binaries for Windows" section
    • Download two files
      • sqlite-tools-win32-x86-*.zip (contains sqlite3.exe command-line tool)
      • sqlite-dll-win64-x64-*.zip (contains the DLL library)
    • Both files will download to your Downloads folder
  2. Create SQLite Directory
    • Press Windows key + E to open File Explorer
    • Navigate to your C: drive
    • Create a new folder called sqlite (full path: C:\sqlite)
  3. Extract SQLite Files
    • Go to your Downloads folder
    • Right-click sqlite-tools-win32-x86-*.zip
    • Select "Extract All..."
    • Change destination to C:\sqlite and click Extract
    • Right-click sqlite-dll-win64-x64-*.zip
    • Select "Extract All..."
    • Change destination to C:\sqlite and click Extract
    • Move all .exe and .dll files from the subfolders directly into C:\sqlite
  4. Add SQLite to System PATH
    • Press Windows key and type "environment variables"
    • Click "Edit the system environment variables"
    • Click the "Environment Variables" button at the bottom
    • In the "System variables" section, find and select "Path"
    • Click "Edit"
    • Click "New"
    • Type C:\sqlite and press Enter
    • Click "OK" on all windows to save
  5. Verify Installation
    • Close any open Command Prompt windows
    • Press Windows key + R, type cmd, press Enter
    • Type: 
      
sqlite3 --version
    • You should see the SQLite version number
  6. Test SQLite (Optional)
    • In Command Prompt, type: 
      
sqlite3
    • You should see the SQLite prompt: sqlite>
    • Try a simple command: 
      
SELECT 'SQLite is working!';
    • You should see the message printed
    • Type .quit and press Enter to exit SQLite
  7. Create a Test Database (Optional)
    • In Command Prompt, navigate to a test folder and create a database: 
      
mkdir C:\test-sqlite
cd C:\test-sqlite
sqlite3 test.db
    • Create a simple table: 
      
CREATE TABLE users (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT);
INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES ('Alice');
SELECT * FROM users;
.quit;
    • You should see the data you inserted

Create a Projects Folder

It's helpful to keep all your coding projects organised in one place. Let's create a dedicated Projects folder:

  1. Open File Explorer
    • Press Windows key + E to open File Explorer
  2. Navigate to Your User Folder
    • Click on "This PC" or "Computer" in the left sidebar
    • Open the C: drive
    • Open the Users folder
    • Open the folder with your username
  3. Create the Projects Folder
    • Right-click in an empty space
    • Select "New" → "Folder"
    • Name it Projects
    • Press Enter

Your Projects folder is now at: C:\Users\YourUsername\Projects

In future lessons, you'll save your coding projects in this folder.

Setting Up the Development Server

To view your web pages as you develop them, you'll need to run a local development server. This is important because some web features don't work when opening HTML files directly in a browser. If you installed the Live Server extension in Visual Studio Code, you can skip this step as that will do the same thing. Using this method lets you run and test your web pages even if you are not using Visual Studio Code.

Create a package.json File

  1. Open Visual Studio Code
  2. Select File → Open Folder
  3. Navigate to and select your Projects folder (C:\Users\YourUsername\Projects)
  4. Create a new file called package.json in the Projects folder
  5. Add the following content to the file:
  6. Save the file (Ctrl + S)

Install http-server

  1. Open the integrated terminal in VS Code by pressing Ctrl + ` (backtick key, usually above Tab)
  2. Make sure you're in your Projects folder (the terminal should show the path)
  3. Type 
    
npm install
    and press Enter
  4. Wait for the installation to complete (this installs http-server locally in your Projects folder)

Start the Development Server

  1. In the same terminal, type npm start and press Enter
  2. You should see a message saying the server is running on http://localhost:8000
  3. Open your web browser and go to http://localhost:8000
  4. You should see a directory listing of your Projects folder

Using the Development Server

  • Keep the terminal open while you're working - the server needs to run continuously
  • When you create HTML files in your Projects folder, you can access them through the browser at http://localhost:8000/filename.html
  • When you make changes to your files and save them, refresh your browser to see the changes
  • To stop the server, press Ctrl + C in the terminal
  • To restart the server later, just run npm start again from your Projects folder

macOS Installation Instructions

The latest version of Apple's macOS is macOS Ventura, but you should be able to use recent versions as well. The lessons have not been tested on earlier versions of macOS, but they may work ok. On the next screens are the instructions for installing the recommended software on your macOS computer.

Visual Studio Code

Check whether Visual Studio Code is already installed:

  • Open Finder
  • Go to the Applications folder
  • If you see Visual Studio Code.app, you have it installed. If not, you need to install it.

If you need to install Visual Studio Code, follow these steps:

  1. Download VS Code from the Official Website
    • Open your web browser
    • Go to https://code.visualstudio.com
    • Click the large blue "Download for Mac" button
    • The file VSCode-darwin-universal.zip will download (usually to your Downloads folder)
  2. Extract the Application
    • Open Finder and go to your Downloads folder
    • Double-click the VSCode-darwin-universal.zip file to extract it
    • You'll see a Visual Studio Code.app file appear
  3. Move to Applications Folder
    • Drag Visual Studio Code.app from Downloads to your Applications folder
    • You can open Applications by pressing Command + Shift + A in Finder
    • Or drag it to the Applications icon in the Finder sidebar
    • This installs VS Code permanently on your Mac
  4. First Launch
    • Open your Applications folder
    • Double-click Visual Studio Code
    • macOS may show a security warning: "Visual Studio Code is an app downloaded from the Internet. Are you sure you want to open it?"
    • Click Open
  5. Add to Dock (Optional)
    • While VS Code is running, right-click its icon in the Dock
    • Select Options → Keep in Dock
    • Now you can launch it quickly from the Dock
  6. Install Command Line Tools (Recommended)
    • In VS Code, press Command + Shift + P to open the Command Palette
    • Type shell command
    • Select "Shell Command: Install 'code' command in PATH"
    • Enter your password if prompted
    • Now you can open VS Code from Terminal by typing code
  7. Verify Installation
    • Open Terminal (Command + Space, type "Terminal")
    • Type code --version and press Enter
    • You should see the VS Code version number

Mozilla Firefox

Check whether Firefox is already installed:

  • Open Finder
  • Go to the Applications folder
  • If you see Firefox.app, you have it installed. If not, you need to install it.

If you need to install Firefox, follow these steps:

  1. Download Firefox from the Official Website
    • Open Safari or another browser
    • Go to https://www.mozilla.org/firefox/
    • Click the blue "Download Firefox" button
    • The file Firefox [version].dmg will download (usually to your Downloads folder)
  2. Open the Disk Image
    • Open Finder and go to your Downloads folder
    • Double-click the Firefox [version].dmg file
    • A window will open showing the Firefox icon and an Applications folder shortcut
  3. Install Firefox
    • Drag the Firefox icon to the Applications folder icon
    • Wait for the copy to complete (takes a few seconds)
    • Firefox is now installed
  4. Eject the Disk Image
    • In Finder's sidebar, find the Firefox disk image
    • Click the eject button next to it
    • You can now delete the .dmg file from Downloads if you wish
  5. First Launch
    • Open your Applications folder
    • Double-click Firefox
    • macOS may show a security warning: "Firefox is an app downloaded from the Internet. Are you sure you want to open it?"
    • Click Open
    • Firefox opens with a Welcome screen
    • You can choose to set Firefox as your default browser (optional)
  6. Add to Dock (Optional)
    • While Firefox is running, right-click its icon in the Dock
    • Select Options → Keep in Dock
    • Now you can launch it quickly from the Dock
  7. Verify Installation
    • In Firefox, click the menu button (three horizontal lines) in the top-right corner
    • Click "Firefox" → "About Firefox"
    • You should see the Firefox version number

Node.js

Check whether Node.js is already installed:

  • Open Terminal (Command + Space, type "Terminal")
  • Type node --version and press Enter
  • If you see a version number, you have Node.js installed.

If you need to install Node.js, you have two options:

Option 1: Download from Official Website (Recommended for Beginners)
  1. Download Node.js
    • Open your browser
    • Go to https://nodejs.org/
    • You'll see two download options:
      • LTS (Long Term Support) - Recommended for most users
      • Current - Latest features, but may be less stable
    • Click the button for LTS
    • A .pkg file will download
  2. Install Node.js
    • Open the downloaded .pkg file
    • Click "Continue" through the installation wizard
    • Accept the license agreement
    • Keep the default installation location
    • Click "Install"
    • Enter your password when prompted
    • Wait for installation to complete
    • Click "Close" when finished
Option 2: Install via Homebrew
  1. Install Homebrew (if not already installed)
    • Open Terminal
    • Check if Homebrew is installed: 
      
brew --version
    • If you get "command not found", install Homebrew with: 
      
/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"
    • Follow the on-screen instructions
  2. Install Node.js with Homebrew
    • Run this command: 
      
brew install node
    • Wait for installation to complete
  3. Verify Installation
    1. Check Node.js and npm
      • Close and reopen Terminal
      • Check Node.js version: 
        
node --version
      • You should see the Node.js version number (e.g., v20.x.x)
      • Check npm (Node Package Manager) version: 
        
npm --version
      • You should see the npm version number
      • Check where Node.js is installed: 
        
which node
    2. Test Node.js
      • Run a simple Node.js command: 
        
node -e "console.log('Node.js is working!')"
      • You should see "Node.js is working!" printed
    3. Create a Simple Test Server (Optional)
      • Create a test file and start a simple server to see Node.js in action: 
        
mkdir ~/test-nodejs && cd ~/test-nodejs
echo "const http = require('http'); http.createServer((req, res) => { res.writeHead(200, {'Content-Type': 'text/html'}); res.end('<h1>Hello from Node.js!</h1>'); }).listen(8000, () => console.log('Server running at http://localhost:8000/'));" > server.js
node server.js
      • Open your browser and go to http://localhost:8000
      • You should see "Hello from Node.js!"
      • Press Control + C in Terminal to stop the server
After installing Node.js
  1. Verify Installation
    1. Check Node.js and npm
      • Close and reopen Terminal
      • Check Node.js version: 
        
node --version
      • You should see the Node.js version number (e.g., v20.x.x)
      • Check npm (Node Package Manager) version: 
        
npm --version
      • You should see the npm version number
      • Check where Node.js is installed: 
        
which node
    2. Test Node.js
      • Run a simple Node.js command: 
        
node -e "console.log('Node.js is working!')"
      • You should see "Node.js is working!" printed
    3. Create a Simple Test Server (Optional)
      • Create a test file and start a simple server to see Node.js in action: 
        
mkdir ~/test-nodejs && cd ~/test-nodejs
echo "const http = require('http'); http.createServer((req, res) => { res.writeHead(200, {'Content-Type': 'text/html'}); res.end('<h1>Hello from Node.js!</h1>'); }).listen(8000, () => console.log('Server running at http://localhost:8000/'));" > server.js
node server.js
      • Open your browser and go to http://localhost:8000
      • You should see "Hello from Node.js!"
      • Press Control + C in Terminal to stop the server

Git

Check whether Git is already installed:

  • Open Terminal (Command + Space, type "Terminal")
  • Type git --version and press Enter
  • If you see a version number, you have it installed. If you get a popup asking to install Command Line Developer Tools, you need to install Git.

If you need to install Git, you have three options

Option 1: Install via Xcode Command Line Tools (Recommended)
  1. Trigger Installation Prompt
    • Open Terminal
    • Type 
      
git --version
      and press Enter
    • If Git is not installed, macOS will show a popup: "The 'git' command requires the command line developer tools. Would you like to install the tools now?"
    • Click "Install"
  2. Installation Process
    • Click "Agree" to the license agreement
    • Wait for the download and installation to complete (this can take 5-15 minutes depending on your internet speed)
    • When complete, click "Done"
  3. Configure Git
    • Set Your Name and Email
      • Open Terminal
      • Set your name and email (replace "Your Name" and "your.email@example.com" with your actual name and email): 
        
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your.email@example.com"
      • These settings will be used to identify you in your Git commits
  4. Verify Installation
    • Type: 
      
git --version
    • You should see the Git version number
    • Check your configuration: 
      
git config --global --list
    • You should see your name and email listed
Option 2: Install via Homebrew
  1. Install Git with Homebrew
    • Open Terminal
    • Run this command: 
      
brew install git
    • Wait for installation to complete
  2. Configure Git
    • Set Your Name and Email
      • Open Terminal
      • Set your name and email (replace "Your Name" with your actual name and "your.email@example.com" with your actual email): 
        
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your.email@example.com"
      • These settings will be used to identify you in your Git commits
  3. Verify Installation
    • Type: 
      
git --version
    • You should see the Git version number
    • Check your configuration: 
      
git config --global --list
    • You should see your name and email listed
Option 3: Download from Git Website
  1. Download Git
    • Open your browser
    • Go to https://git-scm.com/
    • Click "Download for Mac"
    • A .dmg file will download
  2. Install Git
    • Open the downloaded .dmg file
    • Double-click the .pkg file inside
    • Follow the installation wizard
    • Click "Continue" and "Install"
    • Enter your password when prompted
    • Wait for installation to complete
  3. Configure Git
    • Set Your Name and Email
      • Open Terminal
      • Set your name (replace "Your Name" with your actual name): 
        
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
      • Set your email (replace with your actual email): 
        
git config --global user.email "your.email@example.com"
      • These settings will be used to identify you in your Git commits
  4. Verify Installation
    • Type: 
      
git --version
    • You should see the Git version number
    • Check your configuration: 
      
git config --global --list
    • You should see your name and email listed

SQLite

Good News: macOS comes with SQLite pre-installed! You usually don't need to install it separately.

Verify Existing Installation

  1. Check SQLite Version
    • Open Terminal
    • Type: 
      
sqlite3 --version
    • You should see the SQLite version (typically 3.x.x)
Test SQLite
  • Type: 
    
sqlite3
  • You should see the SQLite prompt: sqlite>
  • Try a simple command: 
    
SELECT 'SQLite is working!';
  • You should see the message printed
  • Type .quit and press Enter to exit SQLite

Update SQLite via Homebrew (Optional)

If you want a newer version than what comes with macOS:

  1. Install/Update via Homebrew
    • Open Terminal
    • If you have Homebrew installed: 
      
brew install sqlite3
    • Or to update an existing installation: 
      
brew upgrade sqlite3
  2. Use the Homebrew Version
    • The Homebrew version will be installed to /opt/homebrew/bin/sqlite3 or /usr/local/bin/sqlite3
    • Check which version is being used: 
      
which sqlite3
    • To prefer the Homebrew version, you may need to adjust your PATH in your shell configuration

Create a Test Database (Optional)

  1. Create a Test Directory
    • In Terminal, run: 
      
mkdir ~/test-sqlite && cd ~/test-sqlite
  2. Create a Database
    • Run: 
      
sqlite3 test.db
    • Create a simple table: 
      
CREATE TABLE users (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT);
INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES ('Alice');
SELECT * FROM users;
    • You should see the data you inserted
    • Type .quit to exit
  3. View the Database File
    • List files in the directory: 
      
ls -lh
    • You should see test.db

Create a Projects Folder

It's helpful to keep all your coding projects organised in one place. Let's create a dedicated Projects folder:

  1. Open Finder
    • Click on the Finder icon in the Dock
  2. Navigate to Your Home Folder
    • In the menu bar, click "Go" → "Home"
    • Or press Shift + Cmd + H
  3. Create the Projects Folder
    • Right-click (or Control-click) in an empty space
    • Select "New Folder"
    • Name it Projects
    • Press Enter

Your Projects folder is now at: ~/Projects (or /Users/YourUsername/Projects)

In future lessons, you'll save your coding projects in this folder.

Setting Up the Development Server

To view your web pages as you develop them, you'll need to run a local development server. This is important because some web features don't work when opening HTML files directly in a browser. If you installed the Live Server extension in Visual Studio Code, you can skip this step as that will do the same thing. Using this method lets you run and test your web pages even if you are not using Visual Studio Code.

Create a package.json File

  1. Open Visual Studio Code
  2. Select File → Open Folder
  3. Navigate to and select your Projects folder (~/Projects)
  4. Create a new file called package.json in the Projects folder
  5. Add the following content to the file:
  6. Save the file (Cmd + S)

Install http-server

  1. Open the integrated terminal in VS Code by pressing Ctrl + ` (backtick key, usually above Tab)
  2. Make sure you're in your Projects folder (the terminal should show the path)
  3. Type npm install and press Enter
  4. Wait for the installation to complete (this installs http-server locally in your Projects folder)

Start the Development Server

  1. In the same terminal, type npm start and press Enter
  2. You should see a message saying the server is running on http://localhost:8000
  3. Open your web browser and go to http://localhost:8000
  4. You should see a directory listing of your Projects folder

Using the Development Server

  • Keep the terminal open while you're working - the server needs to run continuously
  • When you create HTML files in your Projects folder, you can access them through the browser at http://localhost:8000/filename.html
  • When you make changes to your files and save them, refresh your browser to see the changes
  • To stop the server, press Ctrl + C in the terminal
  • To restart the server later, just run npm start again from your Projects folder

Linux Installation Instructions

There are many different versions of Linux available, so these instructions will cover the most common distributions: Ubuntu, Fedora and ArchLinux. If you are using a different distribution, the steps may vary slightly. The lessons have been tested on Linux Mint 22.2 (which is based on Ubuntu 22.04) but should work fine with other flavours and versions. On the next screens are the instructions for installing the recommended software on your Linux computer.

Visual Studio Code

Check whether Visual Studio Code is already installed:

  • Open Terminal (usually Ctrl + Alt + T)
  • Type code --version and press Enter
  • If you see a version number, you have it installed. If you get a "command not found" error, you need to install it.

Ubuntu / Debian-based Linux

If you need to install Visual Studio Code on Ubuntu, follow these steps:

  1. Download and Import Microsoft GPG Key
    • Open Terminal
    • Run this command to download and install the Microsoft GPG key: 
      
wget -qO- https://packages.microsoft.com/keys/microsoft.asc | gpg --dearmor > packages.microsoft.gpg
sudo install -D -o root -g root -m 644 packages.microsoft.gpg /etc/apt/keyrings/packages.microsoft.gpg
  2. Add VS Code Repository
    • Run this command to add the VS Code repository: 
      
sudo sh -c 'echo "deb [arch=amd64,arm64,armhf signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/packages.microsoft.gpg] https://packages.microsoft.com/repos/code stable main" > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/vscode.list'
  3. Update Package List and Install
    • Install VS Code: 
      
sudo apt update
sudo apt install code
    • Wait for installation to complete
  4. Launch VS Code
    • Type code in Terminal and press Enter, or
    • Find "Visual Studio Code" in your application menu
    • Click to open VS Code

Fedora / Red Hat-based Linux

If you need to install Visual Studio Code on Fedora, follow these steps:

  1. Import Microsoft GPG Key
    • Open Terminal
    • Run this command: 
      
sudo rpm --import https://packages.microsoft.com/keys/microsoft.asc
  2. Add VS Code Repository
    • Run this command to create the repository file: 
      
sudo sh -c 'echo -e "[code]
name=Visual Studio Code
baseurl=https://packages.microsoft.com/yumrepos/vscode
enabled=1
gpgcheck=1
gpgkey=https://packages.microsoft.com/keys/microsoft.asc" > /etc/yum.repos.d/vscode.repo'
  3. Update Package Cache and Install
    • For Fedora 22 and later: 
      
sudo dnf check-update
sudo dnf install code
    • For older versions: 
      
sudo yum check-update
sudo yum install code
    • Wait for installation to complete
  4. Launch VS Code
    • Type code in Terminal and press Enter, or
    • Find "Visual Studio Code" in your application menu
    • Click to open VS Code

Alternative: Download .deb or .rpm Package

You can also download VS Code directly from the website:

  1. Download from Official Website
    • Open your web browser
    • Go to https://code.visualstudio.com
    • Click "Download" and select:
      • .deb for Ubuntu/Debian
      • .rpm for Fedora/Red Hat
  2. Install the Package
    • Navigate to your Downloads folder
    • For Ubuntu/Debian: Double-click the .deb file and click "Install", or run: 
      
sudo dpkg -i code_*.deb
sudo apt-get install -f
    • For Fedora: Double-click the .rpm file, or run: 
      
sudo dnf install ./code-*.rpm
    • For Arch Linux: Install using an AUR helper like yay or paru: 
      
yay -S visual-studio-code-bin
      Or download from the Arch repositories

Mozilla Firefox

Check whether Firefox is already installed:

  • Open Terminal (usually Ctrl + Alt + T)
  • Type firefox --version and press Enter
  • If you see a version number, you have it installed. If you get a "command not found" error, you need to install it.

Ubuntu / Debian-based Linux

If you need to install Firefox on Ubuntu, follow these steps:

  1. Update Package List
    • Open Terminal
    • Run this command to update your package list: 
      
sudo apt update
    • Enter your password when prompted
  2. Install Firefox
    • Run this command: 
      
sudo apt install firefox
    • Type Y when asked to confirm
    • Wait for installation to complete
  3. Launch Firefox
    • Type firefox in Terminal and press Enter, or
    • Find "Firefox" in your application menu
    • Click to open Firefox
  4. Verify Installation
    • In Firefox, click the menu button (three horizontal lines) in the top-right corner
    • Click "Help" → "About Firefox"
    • You should see the Firefox version number

Fedora / Red Hat-based Linux

If you need to install Firefox on Fedora, follow these steps:

  1. Install Firefox
    • Open Terminal
    • For Fedora 22 and later: 
      
sudo dnf install firefox
    • For older versions: 
      
sudo yum install firefox
    • Type Y when asked to confirm
    • Wait for installation to complete
  2. Launch Firefox
    • Type firefox in Terminal and press Enter, or
    • Find "Firefox" in your application menu
    • Click to open Firefox
  3. Verify Installation
    • In Firefox, click the menu button (three horizontal lines) in the top-right corner
    • Click "Help" → "About Firefox"
    • You should see the Firefox version number

Arch Linux

If you need to install Firefox on Arch Linux, follow these steps:

  1. Install Firefox
    • Open Terminal
    • Run this command: 
      
sudo pacman -S firefox
    • Type Y when asked to confirm
    • Wait for installation to complete
  2. Launch Firefox
    • Type firefox in Terminal and press Enter, or
    • Find "Firefox" in your application menu
    • Click to open Firefox

Alternative: Download from Mozilla Website

You can also download Firefox directly from the website:

  1. Download from Official Website
    • Open your web browser
    • Go to https://www.mozilla.org/firefox/
    • Click "Download Firefox"
    • A .tar.bz2 file will download
  2. Extract and Install
    • Navigate to your Downloads folder
    • Extract the archive: 
      
tar xjf firefox-*.tar.bz2
sudo mv firefox /opt/
sudo ln -s /opt/firefox/firefox /usr/local/bin/firefox
  3. Launch Firefox
    • Type firefox in Terminal and press Enter

Node.js

Check whether Node.js is already installed:

  • Open Terminal (usually Ctrl + Alt + T)
  • Type node --version and press Enter
  • If you see a version number, you have it installed. If you get a "command not found" error, you need to install it.

Ubuntu / Debian-based Linux

If you need to install Node.js on Ubuntu, follow these steps:

  1. Install Node.js via NodeSource Repository (Recommended)
    • Open Terminal
    • Run these commands: 
      
sudo apt update
sudo apt install curl
curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_lts.x | sudo -E bash -
sudo apt install nodejs
    • Wait for installation to complete
  2. Alternative: Install from Ubuntu Repositories
    • Note: This may install an older version
    • Open Terminal
    • Run: 
      
sudo apt update
sudo apt install nodejs npm
  3. Verify Installation
    1. Check Node.js and npm
      • Open a new Terminal window
      • Check Node.js version: 
        
node --version
      • You should see the Node.js version number (e.g., v20.x.x)
      • Check npm version: 
        
npm --version
      • You should see the npm version number
    2. Test Node.js
      • Run a simple command: 
        
node -e "console.log('Node.js is working!')"
      • You should see "Node.js is working!" printed
    3. Create a Simple Test Server (Optional)
      • Create a test file and start a simple server: 
        
mkdir ~/test-nodejs && cd ~/test-nodejs
echo "const http = require('http'); http.createServer((req, res) => { res.writeHead(200, {'Content-Type': 'text/html'}); res.end('<h1>Hello from Node.js!</h1>'); }).listen(8000, () => console.log('Server running at http://localhost:8000/'));" > server.js
node server.js
      • Open your browser and go to http://localhost:8000
      • You should see "Hello from Node.js!"
      • Press Ctrl + C in Terminal to stop the server

Fedora / Red Hat-based Linux

If you need to install Node.js on Fedora:

  1. Install Node.js
    • Open Terminal
    • For Fedora 22 and later: 
      
sudo dnf install nodejs npm
    • For older versions: 
      
sudo yum install nodejs npm
    • Wait for installation to complete
  2. Verify Installation
    1. Check Node.js and npm
      • Open a new Terminal window
      • Check Node.js version: 
        
node --version
      • You should see the Node.js version number (e.g., v20.x.x)
      • Check npm version: 
        
npm --version
      • You should see the npm version number
    2. Test Node.js
      • Run a simple command: 
        
node -e "console.log('Node.js is working!')"
      • You should see "Node.js is working!" printed
    3. Create a Simple Test Server (Optional)
      • Create a test file and start a simple server: 
        
mkdir ~/test-nodejs && cd ~/test-nodejs
echo "const http = require('http'); http.createServer((req, res) => { res.writeHead(200, {'Content-Type': 'text/html'}); res.end('<h1>Hello from Node.js!</h1>'); }).listen(8000, () => console.log('Server running at http://localhost:8000/'));" > server.js
node server.js
      • Open your browser and go to http://localhost:8000
      • You should see "Hello from Node.js!"
      • Press Ctrl + C in Terminal to stop the server

Arch Linux

If you're using Arch Linux:

  1. Install Node.js
    • Open Terminal
    • Run: 
      
sudo pacman -S nodejs npm
    • Wait for installation to complete
  2. Verify Installation
    1. Check Node.js and npm
      • Open a new Terminal window
      • Check Node.js version: 
        
node --version
      • You should see the Node.js version number (e.g., v20.x.x)
      • Check npm version: 
        
npm --version
      • You should see the npm version number
    2. Test Node.js
      • Run a simple command: 
        
node -e "console.log('Node.js is working!')"
      • You should see "Node.js is working!" printed
    3. Create a Simple Test Server (Optional)
      • Create a test file and start a simple server: 
        
mkdir ~/test-nodejs && cd ~/test-nodejs
echo "const http = require('http'); http.createServer((req, res) => { res.writeHead(200, {'Content-Type': 'text/html'}); res.end('<h1>Hello from Node.js!</h1>'); }).listen(8000, () => console.log('Server running at http://localhost:8000/'));" > server.js
node server.js
      • Open your browser and go to http://localhost:8000
      • You should see "Hello from Node.js!"
      • Press Ctrl + C in Terminal to stop the server

Using Node Version Manager (nvm) - Advanced Option

For more control over Node.js versions:

  1. Install nvm
    • Run this command: 
      
curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.39.0/install.sh | bash
    • Close and reopen your terminal
  2. Install Node.js with nvm
    • Install the LTS version: 
      
nvm install --lts
    • Set it as default: 
      
nvm use --lts
  3. Verify Installation
    1. Check Node.js and npm
      • Open a new Terminal window
      • Check Node.js version: 
        
node --version
      • You should see the Node.js version number (e.g., v20.x.x)
      • Check npm version: 
        
npm --version
      • You should see the npm version number
    2. Test Node.js
      • Run a simple command: 
        
node -e "console.log('Node.js is working!')"
      • You should see "Node.js is working!" printed
    3. Create a Simple Test Server (Optional)
      • Create a test directory: 
        
mkdir ~/test-nodejs && cd ~/test-nodejs
      • Create a test file: 
        
echo "const http = require('http'); http.createServer((req, res) => { res.writeHead(200, {'Content-Type': 'text/html'}); res.end('<h1>Hello from Node.js!</h1>'); }).listen(8000, () => console.log('Server running at http://localhost:8000/'));" > server.js
      • Start the server: 
        
node server.js
      • Open your browser and go to http://localhost:8000
      • You should see "Hello from Node.js!"
      • Press Ctrl + C in Terminal to stop the server

Git

Check whether Git is already installed:

  • Open Terminal (usually Ctrl + Alt + T)
  • Type git --version and press Enter
  • If you see a version number, you have it installed. If you get a "command not found" error, you need to install it.

Ubuntu / Debian-based Linux

If you need to install Git on Ubuntu, follow these steps:

  1. Update Package List
    • Open Terminal
    • Run this command to update your package list: 
      
sudo apt update
    • Enter your password when prompted
  2. Install Git
    • Run this command: 
      
sudo apt install git
    • Type Y when asked to confirm
    • Wait for installation to complete
  3. Configure Git
    1. Set Your Name and Email
      • Open Terminal
      • Set your name and email (replace "Your Name" with your actual name and "your.email@example.com" with your actual email): 
        
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your.email@example.com"
      • These settings will be used to identify you in your Git commits
    2. Verify Installation
      • Type: 
        
git --version
      • You should see the Git version number
      • Check your configuration: 
        
git config --global --list
      • You should see your name and email listed
    3. Optional: Configure Default Branch Name

      Modern Git practices use "main" instead of "master" as the default branch name. To set this globally:

      
git config --global init.defaultBranch main

Fedora / Red Hat-based Linux

If you need to install Git on Fedora, follow these steps:

  1. Install Git
    • Open Terminal
    • For Fedora 22 and later: 
      
sudo dnf install git
    • For older versions: 
      
sudo yum install git
    • Type Y when asked to confirm
    • Wait for installation to complete
  2. Configure Git
    1. Set Your Name and Email
      • Open Terminal
      • Set your name and email (replace "Your Name" with your actual name and "your.email@example.com" with your actual email): 
        
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your.email@example.com"
      • These settings will be used to identify you in your Git commits
    2. Verify Installation
      • Type: 
        
git --version
      • You should see the Git version number
      • Check your configuration: 
        
git config --global --list
      • You should see your name and email listed
    3. Optional: Configure Default Branch Name

      Modern Git practices use "main" instead of "master" as the default branch name. To set this globally:

      
git config --global init.defaultBranch main

Arch Linux

If you're using Arch Linux:

  1. Install Git
    • Open Terminal
    • Run this command: 
      
sudo pacman -S git
    • Type Y when asked to confirm
    • Wait for installation to complete
  2. Configure Git
    1. Set Your Name and Email
      • Open Terminal
      • Set your name (replace "Your Name" with your actual name): 
        
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
      • Set your email (replace with your actual email): 
        
git config --global user.email "your.email@example.com"
      • These settings will be used to identify you in your Git commits
    2. Verify Installation
      • Type: 
        
git --version
      • You should see the Git version number
      • Check your configuration: 
        
git config --global --list
      • You should see your name and email listed
    3. Optional: Configure Default Branch Name

      Modern Git practices use "main" instead of "master" as the default branch name. To set this globally:

      
git config --global init.defaultBranch main

SQLite

Check whether SQLite is already installed:

  • Open Terminal (usually Ctrl + Alt + T)
  • Type sqlite3 --version and press Enter
  • If you see a version number, you have it installed. If you get a "command not found" error, you need to install it.

Note: Many Linux distributions come with SQLite pre-installed, but if not, it's easy to install.

Ubuntu / Debian-based Linux

If you need to install SQLite on Ubuntu, follow these steps:

  1. Install SQLite
    • Run this command: 
      
sudo apt update
sudo apt install sqlite3
    • Enter your password when prompted
    • Type Y when asked to confirm
    • Wait for installation to complete
  2. Install SQLite Development Files (Optional)
    • If you plan to develop with SQLite or use it with programming languages: 
      
sudo apt install libsqlite3-dev
  3. Verify Installation (All Distributions):
    1. Check Version
      • In Terminal, type: 
        
sqlite3 --version
      • You should see the SQLite version (typically 3.x.x)
    2. Test SQLite
      • Type: 
        
sqlite3
      • You should see the SQLite prompt: sqlite>
      • Try a simple command: 
        
SELECT 'SQLite is working!';
.quit
      • You should see the message printed
    3. Create a Test Database (Optional):
      1. Create a Test database
        • In Terminal, type: 
          
mkdir ~/test-sqlite
 cd ~/test-sqlite
 sqlite3 test.db
      2. Create a Database
        • Create a simple table: 
          
CREATE TABLE users (id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, name TEXT);
INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES ('Alice');
SELECT * FROM users;
.quit;
        • You should see the data you inserted
      3. View the Database File
        • List files in the directory: 
          
ls -lh
        • You should see test.db

Fedora / Red Hat-based Linux

If you need to install SQLite on Fedora, follow these steps:

  1. Install SQLite
    • Open Terminal
    • For Fedora 22 and later: 
      
sudo dnf install sqlite
    • For older versions: 
      
sudo yum install sqlite
    • Type Y when asked to confirm
    • Wait for installation to complete
  2. Install Development Files (Optional)
    • If you plan to develop with SQLite: 
      
sudo dnf install sqlite-devel
    • Or for older versions: 
      
sudo yum install sqlite-devel

Arch Linux

If you're using Arch Linux:

  1. Install SQLite
    • Open Terminal
    • Run this command: 
      
sudo pacman -S sqlite
    • Type Y when asked to confirm
    • Wait for installation to complete

Useful SQLite Commands

Once you're in the SQLite prompt, here are some helpful commands:

  • .help - Show help information
  • .tables - List all tables in the database
  • .schema - Show the database schema
  • .quit or .exit - Exit SQLite
  • .mode column - Display results in column format
  • .headers on - Show column headers

Create a Projects Folder

It's helpful to keep all your coding projects organised in one place. Let's create a dedicated Projects folder:

  1. Open Terminal
    • Press Ctrl + Alt + T
  2. Navigate to Your Home Directory
    • The terminal usually starts in your home directory
    • To confirm, type pwd and press Enter
    • You should see something like /home/yourusername
  3. Create the Projects Folder
    • Type this command and press Enter: 
      
mkdir Projects
    • Verify it was created: 
      
ls
    • You should see Projects in the list

Your Projects folder is now at: ~/Projects (or /home/yourusername/Projects)

In future lessons, you'll save your coding projects in this folder.

Setting Up the Development Server

+

To view your web pages as you develop them, you'll need to run a local development server. This is important because some web features don't work when opening HTML files directly in a browser. If you installed the Live Server extension in Visual Studio Code, you can skip this step as that will do the same thing. Using this method lets you run and test your web pages even if you are not using Visual Studio Code.

Create a package.json File

  1. Open Visual Studio Code
  2. Select File → Open Folder
  3. Navigate to and select your Projects folder (~/Projects)
  4. Create a new file called package.json in the Projects folder
  5. Add the following content to the file:
  6. Save the file (Ctrl + S)

Install http-server

  1. Open the integrated terminal in VS Code by pressing Ctrl + ` (backtick key, usually above Tab)
  2. Make sure you're in your Projects folder (the terminal should show the path)
  3. Type npm install and press Enter
  4. Wait for the installation to complete (this installs http-server locally in your Projects folder)

Start the Development Server

  1. In the same terminal, type npm start and press Enter
  2. You should see a message saying the server is running on http://localhost:8000
  3. Open your web browser and go to http://localhost:8000
  4. You should see a directory listing of your Projects folder

Using the Development Server

  • Keep the terminal open while you're working - the server needs to run continuously
  • When you create HTML files in your Projects folder, you can access them through the browser at http://localhost:8000/filename.html
  • When you make changes to your files and save them, refresh your browser to see the changes
  • To stop the server, press Ctrl + C in the terminal
  • To restart the server later, just run npm start again from your Projects folder

Conclusion

Congratulations! You have installed all the necessary tools to start your web development journey. In the next lesson, you will create your first web page using HTML and CSS.
